external.description: Aruna Gobalan, a 34-year-old software engineer and recent immigrant to California, was a new mother just returning from maternity leave when she began experiencing unexplained symptoms that puzzled doctors. After numerous tests, including a biopsy prompted by a drooping eyelid, she was diagnosed with stage 4 non-Hodgkin’s lymphoma with extensive dural involvement. The prognosis was grim: she was given only three months to live. Facing the challenges of aggressive treatment while caring for her infant daughter and managing a demanding job without nearby family support, Aruna endured eight cycles of traditional chemotherapy and six cycles of intrathecal chemotherapy delivered directly into her spinal canal. The treatment caused severe side effects, including significant weight loss, mouth sores, nausea, and hair loss. Despite the odds, she achieved remission after her 14th chemo cycle and celebrated her daughter’s first birthday. Motivated by a promise to care for her body if she survived, Aruna transformed her lifestyle. She began running, progressing from her first 10K to completing 26 marathons over the years. At her 10-year follow-up, her oncologist considered her incurable cancer cured. Now in her 50s, Aruna reflects on survivor’s guilt, the limitations of language in facing terminal illness, and credits modern medical science for her survival. She continues to advocate for scientific progress in healthcare while cherishing everyday moments with her family.
external.title: A 34-Year-Old New Mother Diagnosed With Stage 4 Non-Hodgkin Lymphoma Beats 3-Month Prognosis Through Chemotherapy and Becomes a Marathon Runner

Companies Explore Space-Based AI Computing to Process Data Closer to Source

🤖 IA: It's not clickbait ✅
👥 Usuarios: It's not clickbait ✅

#ai #edgecomputing #satellites 

View full AI summary:

external.description: Tech companies and startups are increasingly developing orbital data centers to process AI-generated data directly in space, aiming to reduce latency and energy consumption. This approach, known as edge computing, allows satellites to prioritize, compress, and transmit critical data efficiently, enhancing mission autonomy and enabling faster insights for areas such as defense, agriculture, climate monitoring, and disaster response. Industry leaders including Hewlett Packard Enterprise, Pixxel, Skyroot Aerospace, Dhruva Space, SatSure, and Digantara are pioneering systems that integrate onboard computing with ground-based analysis. These innovations allow satellites to make real-time decisions in orbit while maintaining reliance on terrestrial infrastructure for deeper analysis. Notably, Digantara plans a constellation of 15 satellites by 2027 for space domain awareness, while HPE's Spaceborne Computer demonstrates the feasibility of high-performance computing in extreme space conditions. Executives highlight that in-orbit AI must manage strict power, thermal, and computational constraints, making selective processing and prioritization essential. Experts stress that space-based computing should be integrated into the overall mission architecture to optimize latency, bandwidth, and data sovereignty. This trend represents a crucial evolution in AI-powered data processing, extending cloud and edge infrastructure beyond Earth.
external.title: Companies Explore Space-Based AI Computing to Process Data Closer to Source

Governance Alignment: The imperative to establish frameworks and policies that ensure all AI development and deployment reflect human values and priorities, preventing misuse or unintended consequences.
These perspectives set the stage for understanding why AI ethics and responsibility matter—the field emerged precisely because technologists recognized the need to build safeguards into AI systems from the ground up.

🔍 Understanding the Key Idea
The most critical concept to grasp is why the X-Riscus community insists on caution despite AI's tremendous potential. Their argument isn't that AI is inherently evil; rather, they contend that without deliberate alignment with human values during development, powerful systems could produce unintended consequences at scale.

The goal is to make sure that all we do with AI aligns with human values.
This statement encapsulates the ethical foundation of responsible AI development. When AI systems learn from data or operate autonomously, they can amplify biases, make decisions humans didn't anticipate, or optimize for metrics misaligned with broader societal good. The X-Riscus perspective argues that proactive governance—not reactive regulation after problems emerge—is essential.

Progress (in AI context)

The pace of advancement in AI capabilities—how quickly systems become more powerful, autonomous, and capable of handling complex tasks. The debate centers on whether rapid progress is beneficial without accompanying ethical frameworks.

Perspective	Core Belief	Primary Focus
X-Riscus Approach	Cautious acceleration with governance oversight	Alignment with human values, existential risk mitigation
Implied Alternative	Rapid progress with market-driven innovation	Speed to benefit, competitive advantage
Understanding this tension is foundational: ethics in AI isn't about slowing progress but ensuring it happens thoughtfully.

Der Manga »Shi ni Modori Reijou no Lucetta« von Shinobu Amano ist am 24. März 2026 in der Ausgabe 5/2026 des »LaLa« Magazin von Hakusensha zu Ende gegangen. Die Reihe lief in Japan von September 2022 bis März 2026 und umfasst aktuell 5 Bände. Der sechste und zugleich finale Band soll im Herbst 2026 erscheinen. Eine englische Veröffentlichung der einzelnen Kapitel erscheint bei Comikey, während die Reihe hierzulande bislang nicht auf Deutsch erhältlich ist.

📜 Beschreibung 》
Die verarmte Adlige Lucetta soll den reichen Emporkömmling Kyle heiraten, damit ihre Familie ihre Schulden loswird. Doch ihre erste Ehe endet in Streit, Kälte und schließlich mit ihrem Tod. Als sie plötzlich an den Beginn ihrer Verlobung zurückkehrt, will sie alles ändern. Um die Verbindung zu lösen, nutzt sie ihr Wissen aus der Zukunft und arbeitet heimlich als Wahrsagerin. Doch ausgerechnet Kyle taucht bei ihr auf, um sich in Liebesfragen beraten zu lassen.

record.text: Early career hires in the industries most exposed to AI dropped by 9% after ChatGPT's release, explaining a 12% decline in employment through early 2025.

images.alt: Using detailed tabulations from matched employer-employee administrative data, I document evidence of an immediate, sizable, and persistent decrease in the level of early career (22-24 year old) hires following introduction of ChatGPT within the industry-state cells that are most exposed to AI. The decline in hires is the primary cause of large observed declines in employment over the subsequent period. Regression adjusted employment of early career workers in the most AIexposed quintile of industry-state cells declined by 12% over the 10 quarters following the introduction of ChatGPT, even as employment in less exposed industries has remained stable. The rate of hiring largely recovered by early 2025, attributable to a smaller employment base. Earnings growth of early career workers in the most exposed industries slowed slightly relative to those in less exposed industries. Although the most AI-exposed quintile of detailed industries is dominated by a handful of industry sectors, I find that the association of higher AI exposure with reduced early career employment and fewer hires is observed across most sectors of the economy. Timing of effects in event studies is consistent with an immediate effect on hiring following introduction of ChatGPT. However, triple difference estimates provide some evidence of earlier trend shifts on employment, hiring, and separations around the onset of the COVID pandemic. I discuss potential explanations, including the increase in remote work and increased educational attainment among workers in AI-exposed occupations. Nonetheless, job gains to early career workers and backfill hires show evidence of discontinuous decline at the time of ChatGPT’s release in comparison to older workers in the same industries. A local projections analysis at the NAICS industry group level shows that industries with high AI exposure are not particularly sensitive to unexpected fluctuations in monetary policy on average relative to other industries in em…

Figure 7: Event study regressions: Hires and separations of early career workers

Note: Each plotted series is estimated from a separate event study regression of the indicated outcome for ages 22-24 on a period by most-exposed quintile indicator. The reference period is 2022q3 for end-of-quarter hires and hiring rate, and 2022q4 for beginning-of-quarter separations and separation rate. Text at top right shows the coefficient for the latest available period, and the last four quarters compared to the four quarters ending in the reference period. Standard errors are two-way clustered by detailed industry and state.

record.text: This paper explores sources of misallocation in manufacturing using a decomposition of productivity dispersion into profitability margin and input mix components.

images.alt: We explore sources of measured misallocation using establishment data from U.S. manufacturing industries. We decompose standard revenue productivity dispersion statistics into contributions by dispersion in revenue margins over costs and dispersion in input cost shares across plants. We establish a formal link between these components and measured allocative efficiency. The results indicate the components contribute similarly to apparent rising misallocation in US manufacturing. We use the mapping between distortions that influence these distinct components to explore the relationship between inferred distortions and mechanisms that influence one or both sources of revenue productivity dispersion. Finally, we show rising misallocation in the US manufacturing sector in the last several decades is pervasive, and yet a few industries account for over half of the aggregate decline.

images.alt:
This is a recurring theme when you try out new AI tools. You recognize that there's a lot that might be done with them, but not much comes to you. You see this in the rise of AI coding tools, which you find extraordinarily impressive as you use them to ... make yourself another ….. news reader? Notes app? Personal website, again? The tool you made for ripping and labeling files out of a popular music service gave you a slight, Napster-ish illicit thrill and actually worked, but your use case — putting them on an old MP3 player to run with — was an aspirational mirage, a task invented to have one.
You ask if this is a failure of imagination, a personality flaw, a matter of creativity or practice, and if you're just sitting in front of a piano that you don't yet know how to play (some of your programmer friends don't have this problem, they say, and are swimming in bespoke apps of their own creation).
You also ask, perhaps, if your inability to find little software-shaped problems to solve in your life is rooted in psychology and related to an awareness that, from the outside, your entire job looks an awful lot like someone else's software-shaped problem (the first thing public LLMs could do, after all, was produce novel, readable piles of text).

You also dimly comprehend that in trying to understand your daily habits as a series of workflows with an eye on automation, you're going through a similar set of motions as countless thousands of companies across the economy, some of whom see nothing but opportunity in AI — to cut costs and people, or to invest and grow — while others, fearing competition and obsolescence, rush to adopt AI without knowing what problems they need to solve, much less which ones the technology can handle. You identify on an emotional level with the doomed firms buying compute they don't really know how to use.
You notice that OpenClaw has you identifying with firms.

record.text: the hottest trend of 2026 is feeding yourself into a machine you don't understand, and which doesn't understand you nymag.com/intelligence...

images.alt:
It is made clear to you, if you didn't already know, that installing OpenClaw is basically a giant, intentional self-hack: a way to see what AI models now capable of manipulating software tools, most notably in tools like Claude Code and Codex, might be able to get done if given the keys to a civilian-grade email-and-document machine.
You're asked to choose how you'd like to talk to your bot, and because it's the app mentioned in most of the online testimonies, you choose Telegram, which from experience you mostly associate with scams, spam, and, lately, the AI propaganda of the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps of Iran.
This is your bot, which is to say your computer, which is to say, well, ChatGPT in a costume with prosthetic hands that can touch your keyboard and move your mouse. It asks you to assign a vibe, give it your time zone, and describe what sort of personality it should have. It all reminds you of setting up a video game. You tell it to be matter-of-fact and concise. You spend a while installing plug-ins that allow OpenClaw to actually take action on your machine, and "Skills," or, essentially, written, repeatable instructions for the agent. In order to set up OpenClaw, an AI chat interface for your computer, you spent a lot of time in the command line, which is how people had to interact with computers before you were born. You note that we've moved from issuing commands to computers to clicking on computers to, now, chatting and haggling with computers, which doesn't sound as obviously empowering as you wish it did, human-agency-wise.

You search X, a political influence project run by Elon Musk where people also talk about AI, and where OpenClaw has for some time been a hot subject. An anonymous account called Big Brain AI - bio: "Learn not to get left behind when AI takes over" — is quoting an interview with OpenClaw's founder. "If you don't navigate them well, if you don't have a vision of what you're going to build, it's still going to be slop," the founder says. Big Brain editorializes in LLM voice: "The agentic trap is what happens when you remove yourself from the process too early." You're failing your own system, in other words.
Skill issue. You're NGMI. In response to Big Brain's post, the head of start-up incubator Y Combinator, who has Sam Altman's old job, chimes in. "This is 100% correct. I experience this 10 hours a day now."
I'm turning my OpenClaw into a Vannevar Bush Memex. It's just going to remember everything I care about and read and it will become my second brain.
Books, writings, research, all of it will be in my personal knowledge wiki and usable for helping me think. pic.twitter.com/lcfxMRnfTq
- Garry Tan (@garrytan) April 7, 2026
You grant that you might just be out of your depth here. But you're also curious if he has people in his life who can check to make sure he's okay.

record.text: 📣 New Podcast! "Sullivan’s Crossing Season 4 Episode 6 | Falling Through the Cracks and the Tale of the literal Ceiling" on @Spreaker

external.description:
It’s Season 4, Episode 6 of Sullivan’s Crossing, titled "Falling Through the Cracks," and the snark squad is heading back to the campsite where "running away from your problems" just became the main event. We’re unpacking a frantic search for the siblings, Ben and Tracy, who decided that a life in the woods was better than being separated by social services. We’re asking: are these kids tactical geniuses for outrunning Cal and Maggie for half the episode, or is the Crossing just the easiest place in the world to get lost in? While the community was busy organizing a search party, we were dissecting a plot that culminated in a literal and metaphorical "fall," as the kids were eventually apprehended by authorities after a tense confrontation in a crawlspace. We’re asking: is the central mystery of their mother's location ever going to be solved, or are the writers just letting that plot point "fall through the cracks" too?


The drama reached a "Passionate Peak" as we analyze the fallout of Liam’s trauma-dump. We’re deep-diving into the long-awaited explanation for his multi-year disappearance—apparently, his "assignment" in a war zone went south, leading to him being held captive. We’re asking: does a sad story and a few physical scars justify him showing up to disrupt Maggie’s "happily ever after" with Cal, or is he just a master manipulator who knows exactly which heartstrings to pull? While Maggie was busy struggling to reconcile her unresolved feelings—leading to a kiss that has the #TeamCal fans on Reddit absolutely spiraling—Cal was busy feeling the "Open Wounds" of jealousy. Between the secondary characters doing community service to distract themselves from the drama and the episode ending with Maggie’s integrity in question, the stable ground at the Crossing is officially shaking.
external.title: Sullivan’s Crossing Season 4 Episode 6 | Falling Through the Cracks and the Tale of the literal Ceiling

record.text: 📣 New Podcast! "Unexpected Season 7 Episode 11 | Birth Certificate Bans and the Tale of Bryce’s Legal Erasure" on @Spreaker

external.description:
It’s Season 7, Episode 11 of Unexpected, titled "Co-Parenting Your Baby," and the snark squad is unpacking a week where the "picket fence" dreams are officially meeting the reality of shared custody and strict house rules. We’re deep-diving into Isabella and Bryce, where the "honeymoon phase" of their engagement has been replaced by the cold, hard ink of a legal document. We’re asking: is Isabella’s decision to exclude Bryce from the birth certificate a move for protection, or is she just manifesting a "single-mom-by-choice" vibe while he’s standing right there? While Bryce is busy asking to meet his newborn daughter—and dealing with the realization that he has zero legal standing—the families are still locked in a cold war that even therapy can’t seem to thaw.

The drama reaches a "Cardinal Rule" peak as we analyze Mia and Jesse’s living situation. In a shocking pivot, Mia’s dad, Keith, has officially softened his stance, allowing Jesse to move into the house—effectively retiring his "no boys in the bedroom" rule in the face of a devastating house fire. We’re asking: is Keith being a supportive grandpa, or is he just tired of fighting a losing battle against "shacking up"? Meanwhile, Amya is heading back to the site of her "go-kart humiliation" for a shot at redemption, proving that her personal health goal is about more than just the scale—it’s about getting her confidence back from a "judgey" personal trainer. And over in Hunter’s world, a promise from his "absentee" father to finally meet baby Wes is sparking cautious optimism. We’re asking: is the dad finally showing up for the right reasons, or is he just trying to save face now that the court drama is officially public? Between Mama G pushing Jose to provide and the long-term stability of these couples looking more like a "Wild West" shootout, the "Unexpected" journey is officially in its most volatile chapter yet.
external.title: Unexpected Season 7 Episode 11 | Birth Certificate Bans and the Tale of Bryce’s Legal Erasure

We’re deep-diving into Isabella and Bryce, where the "honeymoon phase" of their engagement has been replaced by the cold, hard ink of a legal document. We’re asking: is Isabella’s decision to exclude Bryce from the birth certificate a move for protection, or is she just manifesting a "single-mom-by-choice" vibe while he’s standing right there? While Bryce is busy asking to meet his newborn daughter—and dealing with the realization that he has zero legal standing—the families are still locked in a cold war that even therapy can’t seem to thaw.

The drama reaches a "Cardinal Rule" peak as we analyze Mia and Jesse’s living situation. In a shocking pivot, Mia’s dad, Keith, has officially softened his stance, allowing Jesse to move into the house—effectively retiring his "no boys in the bedroom" rule in the face of a devastating house fire. We’re asking: is Keith being a supportive grandpa, or is he just tired of fighting a losing battle against "shacking up"? Meanwhile, Amya is heading back to the site of her "go-kart humiliation" for a shot at redemption, proving that her personal health goal is about more than just the scale—it’s about getting her confidence back from a "judgey" personal trainer. And over in Hunter’s world, a promise from his "absentee" father to finally meet baby Wes is sparking cautious optimism. We’re asking: is the dad finally showing up for the right reasons, or is he just trying to save face now that the court drama is officially public? Between Mama G pushing Jose to provide and the long-term stability of these couples looking more like a "Wild West" shootout, the "Unexpected" journey is officially in its most volatile chapter yet.
external.title: Unexpected Season 7 Episode 11 | Birth Certificate Bans and the Tale of Bryce’s Legal Erasure

record.text: 📣 New Podcast! "Top Chef Season 23 Episode 8 | Southern Comfort and the Tale of the Cheerwine Gel" on @Spreaker

external.description:
It’s Season 23, Episode 8 of Top Chef, and the snark squad is heading into the trenches of the most stressful 36 hours in television: Restaurant Wars. We’re unpacking a "halfway point" showdown that proved you can’t just cook your way out of a bad concept—especially when there’s a "to-go order" gimmick thrown in for maximum chaos. We’re deep-diving into the unexpected return of Sieger Bayer, who bounced back into the competition to fill the gap left by Jennifer’s medical emergency and Justin’s noble (if slightly dramatic) departure. While Sieger was busy trying to find his footing as the Executive Chef for Tierra Reina, the Carolina Queen team was busy slathering their southern comfort food in success. We’re asking: is Laurence a visionary for his "East Meets East" pork loin, or did the team just win because Anthony had immunity and Duyen finally mastered the art of the Front of House without a single "corny" joke?

The drama reached a boiling point as we analyze the "Latin-Carolina fusion" disaster that sent Brandon Dearden packin'. We’re dissecting the "Cheerwine Gel" catastrophe, where Brandon’s Arroz con Leche was dinged for being mediocre and served alongside a massive timing delay that had the judges waiting 40 minutes for a table. We’re asking: is Brandon’s "midcore" dish the reason he’s out, or did he just get caught in the crossfire of a team that couldn't decide if they were serving upscale fusion or a takeout menu? While Brandon headed to the Last Chance Kitchen finale, the real shocker was Rhoda’s total redemption. We’re analyzing the "mini-Restaurant War" where Rhoda defeated Brandon to officially reclaim her spot in the main competition. Between Anthony’s "Banga Broth" saving him despite a weak dish and the technical standard of the Southern concepts being "the best in years," the alliances are shifting faster than a line cook during a rush.
external.title: Top Chef Season 23 Episode 8 | Southern Comfort and the Tale of the Cheerwine Gel

record.text:
Thank you, @Grok – What are AI Deepfakes? 
x.com/i/grok/share...

external.description: AI deepfakes are highly realistic synthetic media—videos, images, audio, or even text—created or manipulated using artificial intelligence, particularly deep learning techniques. The term "deepfake" combines "deep learning" (a subset of machine learning using neural networks) and "fake."  They typically make it appear as if a person is saying or doing something they never did, by swapping faces, altering expressions, synthesizing voices, or generating entirely new content.  How Deepfakes Work Deepfakes rely on advanced AI models like: Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs): Two neural networks compete—one generates fake content, the other tries to detect flaws—improving realism over time. Autoencoders: These compress and reconstruct facial data to swap or manipulate features.  Creators train the AI on large datasets of images/videos of the target person (source) and the person whose likeness is being imposed (target). The result is often indistinguishable from real footage to the casual viewer.  Here are visual examples of deepfake techniques:  History The term emerged in late 2017 when a Reddit user named "deepfakes" posted AI-generated pornographic videos swapping celebrities' faces onto performers' bodies, using open-source tools. Roots trace back to earlier face-swapping tech and GANs introduced in 2014. Accessibility has exploded with user-friendly tools, lowering the barrier for creation.  Uses and Examples Positive/Benign Applications: Entertainment (e.g., digital de-aging in films, historical recreations like a deepfake Nixon speech). Education (simulating historical figures or training scenarios). Accessibility (voice synthesis for those who lost their voice).  Malicious or Controversial Examples: Non-consensual pornography (still the majority of online deepfakes, disproportionately affecting women). Political misinformation (e.g., fake videos of leaders saying inflammatory things). Scams (voice deepfakes impersonating executives to authorize fraudulent transfers; celebrity endorsements for fake investments).  Risks and Concerns Misinformation and eroded trust — Makes it harder to believe video/audio evidence ("crisis of knowing"). Fraud and security — Financial scams, identity theft, corporate espionage (e.g., $25M transfer via video call impersonation). Harassment and defamation — Revenge porn, bullying, political sabotage. Broader impacts — Election interference, privacy violations, potential for fabricated "evidence" in courts or medicine.  Detection tools exist (analyzing inconsistencies in lighting, blinking, audio artifacts), but they lag behind generation tech. Watermarking, blockchain verification, and regulations (e.g., labeling requirements) are emerging countermeasures.  Deepfakes represent a powerful double-edged sword of AI: incredible creative potential alongside serious societal risks. As the technology improves, awareness and verification habits (e.g., cross-checking sources) become essential.
external.title: What are AI Deepfakes?

Full paper from Beatriz Costa Gomes, Pavel Tolmachev, Eloise Taysom, Viknesh Sounderajah and the Microsoft AI Health team:
https://www.nature.com/articles/s44360-026-00117-x

If you're building or governing healthcare AI, this paper is essential reading.

Designing for the user alone misses a substantial portion of how the technology actually gets used.

Caregivers need different information than self-researchers.

An adult asking about an infant's symptoms needs different urgency cues, different context, different follow-up paths than someone tracking their own chronic condition.

One-size personalization fails the people who need accuracy most.
